title_en: Discourse on the True Qi of High Antiquity
paragraphs_en: In former times there was Huangdi (the Yellow Emperor). At birth he was already imbued with spiritual power; while still an infant he could speak; in his childhood he was quick and discerning; grown to youth he was sincere and diligent; and having reached maturity he ascended to the throne. He then asked his Celestial Teacher, saying: 'I have heard that the people of high antiquity all lived past a hundred years, yet their movements did not decline. The people of the present age, however, decline in their capacities by the age of fifty. Is this because the times have changed, or because people themselves have lost the Way?' Qibo replied: 'Among the people of high antiquity, those who understood the Way patterned themselves on yin and yang and lived in harmony with the arts and numbers of self-cultivation. They were moderate in eating and drinking, regular in their rising and resting, and did not labor recklessly. Hence their bodies and spirits remained together, and they were able to live out their allotted heavenly years, departing only after passing a hundred years of age. The people of today are not like this. They take wine as if it were a beverage and treat recklessness as their norm. They enter the bedchamber while intoxicated, letting their desires exhaust their essence and dissipate their true qi. They do not know how to maintain fullness, do not govern their spirit at the proper times, but strive only to please their hearts, going against the joys of life, and are irregular in rising and resting. Hence they decline by the age of fifty.'

Now when the sages of high antiquity taught those below them, they always spoke of vacuous evils and thieving winds, which must be avoided at the proper times. By being tranquil, placid, empty, and desireless, the true qi followed accordingly, and the essence and spirit were guarded within — from where, then, could illness come? Thus their wills were at ease and their desires few; their hearts were calm and without fear; their bodies labored yet did not become weary; their qi followed smoothly, and each person's desires were satisfied, each obtaining what they wished. They found their food delicious, were content with their clothing, delighted in their customs, and did not envy one another whether of high or low station — hence these people were called simple and unadorned. Thus cravings and desires could not weary their eyes, and excessive and deviant influences could not confuse their hearts. Whether foolish or wise, worthy or unworthy, none feared external things, and so they accorded with the Way. The reason they were all able to live beyond a hundred years without their movements declining is that their virtue was whole and undisturbed.

Huangdi said: 'When a person grows old and is without children, is it because their physical capacity is exhausted, or is it simply the natural course of the heavenly numbers?' Qibo said: 'In a girl, at seven years of age, the kidney qi becomes abundant; her teeth change and her hair grows long. At two sevens (fourteen), tiangui — the reproductive essence that matures at puberty and governs fertility — arrives, the Conception Vessel opens, the Penetrating Vessel becomes abundant, and her monthly courses come at their proper time; hence she is able to bear children. At three sevens (twenty-one), the kidney qi is level and balanced, so her wisdom teeth emerge and her growth reaches its fullest extent. At four sevens (twenty-eight), her sinews and bones are firm, her hair reaches its fullest growth, and her body is flourishing and strong. At five sevens (thirty-five), the Yangming vessel declines, her face begins to wither, and her hair begins to fall. At six sevens (forty-two), the three yang vessels decline in the upper body, her face becomes entirely withered, and her hair begins to turn white. At seven sevens (forty-nine), the Conception Vessel becomes deficient, the Penetrating Vessel declines and diminishes, tiangui is exhausted, and the pathway of the earth is no longer open; hence her body deteriorates and she can no longer bear children. In a boy, at eight years of age, the kidney qi becomes replete, his hair grows and his teeth change. At two eights (sixteen), the kidney qi becomes abundant, tiangui arrives, his essence qi overflows and is released, yin and yang become harmonious, and so he is able to father children. At three eights (twenty-four), the kidney qi is level and balanced, his sinews and bones become strong, so his wisdom teeth emerge and his growth reaches its fullest extent. At four eights (thirty-two), his sinews and bones are flourishing and abundant, his flesh and muscles full and strong. At five eights (forty), the kidney qi declines, his hair falls and his teeth wither. At six eights (forty-eight), the yang qi is exhausted and declines in the upper body, his face withers, and his hair and temples turn gray and white. At seven eights (fifty-six), the liver qi declines, his sinews can no longer move freely, tiangui is exhausted, his essence grows scant, his kidney viscus declines, and his body as a whole reaches its limit. At eight eights (sixty-four), his teeth and hair are gone. The kidney governs water; it receives the essence of the five viscera and six bowels and stores it. Hence, when the five viscera are flourishing, the kidney is able to discharge essence in reproduction. But now, when the five viscera have all declined, the sinews and bones become slack and feeble, and tiangui is exhausted. Hence the hair and temples turn white, the body becomes heavy, one's walk becomes unsteady, and one is no longer able to have children.' Huangdi said: 'But there are those who, though already old in years, are still able to have children — why is this?' Qibo said: 'This is because their heavenly allotted lifespan exceeds the ordinary measure, and their qi and vessels remain constantly open and unobstructed, so that their kidney qi still has a surplus. Although such a person may still have children, in a man this will not go beyond eight eights (sixty-four), and in a woman not beyond seven sevens (forty-nine), for by then the essential qi of Heaven and Earth within them will have become entirely exhausted.' Huangdi said: 'Now, those who follow the Way — can they live to a hundred years and still be able to have children?' Qibo said: 'Those who follow the Way are able to hold back old age and keep their bodies whole. Even though they may be advanced in years, they remain able to father children.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that in high antiquity there were Realized Persons (zhenren) who grasped and held Heaven and Earth, who mastered yin and yang, who breathed the essential qi, who stood alone and guarded their spirit, so that their muscles and flesh were as one — hence they could live as long as Heaven and Earth, without any limit or end. This was because they lived according to the Way. In middle antiquity, there were Consummate Persons (zhiren) whose virtue was pure and whose grasp of the Way was complete. They harmonized themselves with yin and yang, attuned themselves to the four seasons, withdrew from the ordinary world and departed from vulgar customs, accumulated their essence and kept their spirit whole, and roamed freely between Heaven and Earth, their sight and hearing extending beyond the eight directions. This is how they increased their years and strengthened themselves, and they too returned to the state of the Realized Persons. Next there were Sages who dwelt in harmony with Heaven and Earth, followed the principles of the eight winds, and adapted their cravings and desires to the ordinary world. They harbored no feelings of resentment or anger; in their conduct they did not wish to separate themselves from the world, yet in their dress and bearing they did not wish to conform to vulgar fashion. Outwardly they did not wear out their bodies with worldly affairs, and inwardly they were free from the burden of anxious thoughts. They made tranquility and contentment their constant task, and self-fulfillment their achievement; their bodies did not become worn out, nor did their essence and spirit scatter, and so they too could live to a hundred years. Next there were Worthies who took Heaven and Earth as their model, patterned themselves on the sun and moon, discerned and arrayed themselves according to the stars and constellations, followed yin and yang in their proper accord and opposition, and distinguished the four seasons. They sought to follow high antiquity and unite themselves with the Way, and so they too were able to increase their lifespan, though theirs had its limit.'

title_en: Great Discourse on Regulating the Spirit in Accord with the Four Seasons' Qi
paragraphs_en: The three months of spring are called the period of unfolding and renewal. Heaven and Earth together bring forth life, and the myriad things flourish. One should retire late and rise early, walk broadly in the courtyard, let the hair hang loose and the body relax, so as to allow the will to be generated. One should give life and not kill, give and not take away, reward and not punish — this is the way of nurturing life in response to the qi of spring. To go against this injures the liver, and in summer this will bring about cold-transmutation disorders, so that one has little with which to support growth. The three months of summer are called the period of luxuriant flourishing. The qi of Heaven and Earth intermingle, and the myriad things bloom and bear fruit. One should retire late and rise early, without growing weary of the long days. One should not let the will become angered, but allow the flower-like brilliance of the body to reach its fullness, allow the qi to be released, as though what one loved were outside oneself — this is the way of nurturing growth in response to the qi of summer. To go against this injures the heart, and in autumn this will bring about recurrent fevers and chills, so that one has little with which to support the gathering-in of autumn, and by the winter solstice one will fall gravely ill. The three months of autumn are called the period of calm equanimity. The qi of Heaven becomes brisk and the qi of Earth becomes clear. One should retire early and rise early, rising together with the crowing of the cock. One should keep the will at peace and tranquil, so as to soften the severity of autumn, gather in the spirit and qi, and cause the autumn qi to be level; one should not let the will extend outward, so as to keep the lung qi clear — this is the way of nurturing the gathering-in in response to the qi of autumn. To go against this injures the lung, and in winter this will bring about diarrhea with undigested food, so that one has little with which to support the storing-up of winter. The three months of winter are called the period of closing and storing. The waters freeze and the earth cracks open; one should not disturb one's yang. One should retire early and rise late, always waiting for the sunlight to appear. One should keep the will as though hidden and concealed, as though harboring a private intention, as though one had already attained something. One should avoid the cold and seek warmth, and not allow the skin to leak and let the qi be repeatedly stolen away — this is the way of nurturing the storing-up in response to the qi of winter. To go against this injures the kidney, and in spring this will bring about wilting and reversal of the limbs, so that one has little with which to support the generation of spring.

The qi of Heaven is pure, tranquil, and luminous. It stores its virtue ceaselessly, and therefore does not descend. If the brightness of Heaven were obscured, then even the sun and moon would lose their brightness, and evil influences would harm the body's openings; the yang qi would become blocked and closed, and the qi of Earth would cloud over what should be bright — the clouds and mist would lose their clarity, so that in response, the white dew above would fail to descend. When the qi of Heaven and Earth no longer communicate and intermingle openly, the life of the myriad things would fail to be bestowed, and if not bestowed, the great trees would largely die. Malign qi would fail to disperse, wind and rain would lose their regulation, the white dew would fail to descend, and dead and withered plants would fail to flourish. Thieving winds would arrive repeatedly, violent rains would arise repeatedly, Heaven and Earth and the four seasons would fail to preserve one another, and, having lost accord with the Way, life would be cut off and extinguished before its time. Only the sage follows the Way of Heaven and Earth; hence his body suffers no strange illness, the myriad things do not lose their due, and the qi of life is never exhausted. To go against the qi of spring causes the lesser yang not to be generated, and the liver qi becomes altered within. To go against the qi of summer causes the greater yang not to grow, and the heart qi becomes hollowed out within. To go against the qi of autumn causes the greater yin not to be gathered in, and the lung qi becomes scorched and full. To go against the qi of winter causes the lesser yin not to be stored, and the kidney qi alone sinks down. Now, yin and yang of the four seasons are the root and foundation of the myriad things. This is why the sage nourishes yang in spring and summer, and nourishes yin in autumn and winter, so as to follow this root; thus he rises and falls together with the myriad things at the gate of growth and development. To go against this root is to hew down this foundation and to ruin one's own true nature.

Thus yin and yang and the four seasons are the beginning and end of the myriad things, the root of death and life. To go against them gives rise to calamity and harm; to follow them prevents severe illness from arising — this is called attaining the Way. The Way is something the sage puts into practice, while the fool merely wears it as an ornament. Following yin and yang brings life; going against them brings death. Following them brings order; going against them brings chaos. To reverse what should be followed is to go against it, and this is called internal obstruction. This is why the sage does not treat illness that has already arisen, but treats illness before it arises; does not bring order to disorder that has already occurred, but brings order before disorder occurs — this is precisely what is meant. For if one only administers medicine after the illness has already formed, or only governs after disorder has already formed, this is like digging a well after one has become thirsty, or casting weapons after the battle has already begun — is this not too late?

title_en: Discourse on How the Generative Qi Communicates with Heaven
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'Since ancient times, communication with Heaven has been the root of life; this root lies in yin and yang. Within Heaven and Earth, within the six directions, the qi of the nine provinces, the nine orifices, the five viscera, and the twelve joints of the limbs — all communicate with the qi of Heaven. It gives birth to the five phases, and its own qi is threefold, the three yin and three yang. Those who repeatedly violate these principles will be injured by evil qi — this is the very root of long life. When the qi of the blue Heaven is pure and tranquil, the will and mind are well ordered; if one accords with this, the yang qi becomes firm, and even though thieving evils may be present, they cannot cause harm — this comes from following the sequence of the seasons. Therefore the sage transmits his essence and spirit, takes in the qi of Heaven, and thereby communicates with spiritual brilliance. If he loses this, then within, the nine orifices become blocked, and without, the flesh and muscles become congested; the defensive qi scatters and dissolves — this is called self-injury, a whittling away of one's qi.'

The yang qi of the body is like Heaven and the sun: if it loses its proper place, one's life is shortened and one's vitality fails to shine forth. Just as the motion of Heaven depends on the brightness of the sun, so does the body's vitality depend on its yang qi. Thus yang qi, by its nature, rises upward and guards the exterior. When affected by cold, the yang qi ought to move like a turning door-pivot; if instead one's rising and resting are as agitated as if startled, the spirit qi will float upward and scatter. When affected by summer-heat, excessive sweating brings panting and gasping; if the person is still, there is much talking; the body feels as if scorched like burning charcoal, and only when sweat is released does the heat disperse. When affected by dampness, the head feels as if wrapped in a cloth; if the damp-heat is not dispelled, the large sinews contract and shorten while the small sinews become slack and lengthen — contraction and shortening cause spasm, while slackness and lengthening cause wilting weakness. When affected by pathogenic qi, swelling occurs; when the four limbs are afflicted in turn, the yang qi becomes exhausted.

When yang qi is overtaxed through vexation and overwork, it becomes overextended; the essence becomes exhausted and accumulates repeatedly, and in summer this causes a person to suffer 'scorching reversal.' The eyes become blind and unable to see, the ears become blocked and unable to hear — the condition collapses like a ruined dam, gushing forth uncontrollably, impossible to stop. When yang qi is affected by great rage, the body's qi becomes cut off, and blood congests in the upper body, causing a person to suffer 'assaulting reversal.' If the sinews are injured and become slack, as though they can no longer contain the body properly, and sweating occurs unevenly on one side, this causes a person to become paralyzed on one side. If sweat is exposed to dampness, boils and heat-rash are produced. Excessive consumption of rich food and fine grains gives rise to large carbuncles in the extremities, striking a person as easily as emptying a vessel. Sweating from exertion, when exposed to wind, and chilled and constricted, produces acne-like eruptions, which when stagnant become boils.

When yang qi is properly refined, it nourishes the spirit; when it is properly soft and pliant, it nourishes the sinews. If the pores' opening and closing lose their proper regulation, cold qi follows and enters, giving rise to severe stooping of the back. When cold sinks into the vessels, it becomes fistula, lingering and lodging in the flesh and interstices. When the qi at the transport points is transformed and becomes attenuated, it is transmitted and becomes excessive fearfulness, and further becomes fright and panic. When the nutritive qi fails to follow its proper course and rebels within the texture of the flesh, this gives rise to welling-abscess and swelling. If the sweat governed by the lung's corporeal soul is not fully discharged, the body becomes weakened and the qi is scorched; the points and transport-holes then close up, and this brings about wind-malaria. Thus wind is the beginning of the hundred diseases. When the body is pure and tranquil, the flesh and interstices remain closed against invasion, so that even in the presence of great winds and severe poisons, one cannot be harmed — this comes from following the sequence of the seasons. Thus when illness lingers long, it is transmitted and transformed into other conditions, and when the upper and lower parts of the body no longer communicate with each other, even a good physician can do nothing. Hence when yang qi accumulates without release, illness leads to death, for the yang qi becomes obstructed; where there is obstruction, one ought to drain it. If proper treatment is not applied swiftly, an unskilled practitioner will only ruin the patient.

Thus, over the course of a single day, the yang qi governs the exterior of the body. At dawn, the human qi is generated; at midday, the yang qi reaches its height; as the sun sets in the west, the yang qi has already become deficient, and the qi gates then close. Therefore, in the evening one should gather in and guard the yang qi, and should not disturb the sinews and bones, nor expose oneself to mist and dew. To act contrary to these three periods of the day will cause the body to become distressed and depleted.

Qibo said: 'Yin stores the essence and rouses it forth repeatedly to respond to yang; yang guards the exterior and secures it firmly. If yin cannot overcome yang, then the pulse flows rapidly and urgently, and when yang qi converges, this results in mania. If yang cannot overcome yin, then the qi of the five viscera contend with one another, and the nine orifices fail to communicate. This is why the sage arranges yin and yang in proper order, so that the sinews and vessels are harmonious, the bones and marrow are firm and solid, and the qi and blood all follow their proper course. When this is so, the interior and exterior are regulated and harmonious, evil qi cannot cause harm, the ears and eyes are sharp and clear, and the qi stands firm as before.'

When wind lodges in the body and overwhelms the qi, the essence is thereby lost, and this is evil injuring the liver. If, in addition, one eats to excess, the sinews and vessels become slack and disordered, and intestinal flux gives rise to hemorrhoids. If one drinks to excess, the qi rebels upward. If one exerts great physical force, the kidney qi is thereby injured, and the great bone of the lower back is thereby damaged. Now, the essential point of yin and yang is this: when yang is dense and secure, it becomes firm. If the two are not in harmony, it is like having spring without autumn, or winter without summer. To bring about their harmony is called the sage's proper measure. Thus if yang is too strong and cannot be kept dense and secure, the yin qi is thereby cut off. When yin is level and balanced and yang is dense and secure, the essence and spirit are well ordered; when yin and yang become separated and severed from each other, the essence qi is thereby exhausted. When the body is affected by exposure to dew and wind, this gives rise to cold and heat disorders.

Thus, if one is injured by wind in spring, the evil qi lingers and lodges within, and this becomes diarrhea with undigested food. If one is injured by summer-heat in summer, in autumn this becomes recurrent malarial fevers. If one is injured by dampness in autumn, qi rebels upward causing cough, and this develops into wilting weakness and reversal. If one is injured by cold in winter, in spring one will certainly develop warm-disease. Thus the qi of the four seasons, in turn, injures the five viscera.

That from which yin essence is generated is rooted in the five flavors; yet the five palaces of yin, the five viscera, can also be injured by the five flavors. Thus, if the flavor sour is consumed to excess, the liver qi becomes overflowing, and the spleen qi is thereby cut off. If the flavor salty is consumed to excess, the qi of the great bones becomes taxed, the muscles are shortened, and the heart qi is repressed. If the flavor sweet is consumed to excess, the heart qi becomes labored and full, the complexion turns black, and the kidney qi loses its balance. If the flavor bitter is consumed to excess, the spleen qi fails to be moistened, and the stomach qi becomes thickened and stagnant. If the flavor pungent is consumed to excess, the sinews and vessels become slack and dissolved, and the essence and spirit are thereby exhausted. Therefore, if one carefully harmonizes the five flavors, the bones will be straight and the sinews supple, the qi and blood will flow freely, and the interstices of the skin will be firm and dense. When this is so, the bone qi will be replete with essence. If one carefully follows the Way according to its proper norms, one will long enjoy the years allotted by Heaven.

title_en: Discourse on the True Words Stored in the Golden Casket
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: 'Heaven has eight winds, and the channels have five winds — what does this mean?' Qibo replied: 'The eight winds give rise to evil qi, which becomes wind within the channels, striking the five viscera, and the evil qi thereby brings forth disease. What is called the overcoming of the four seasons is this: spring overcomes late summer, late summer overcomes winter, winter overcomes summer, summer overcomes autumn, and autumn overcomes spring — this is what is meant by the overcoming relationships among the four seasons. The east wind arises in spring, and its disease is located in the liver, with its transport point at the neck. The south wind arises in summer, and its disease is located in the heart, with its transport point at the chest and rib-side. The west wind arises in autumn, and its disease is located in the lung, with its transport point at the shoulders and back. The north wind arises in winter, and its disease is located in the kidney, with its transport point at the lower back and thighs. The center corresponds to earth, and its disease is located in the spleen, with its transport point at the spine. Thus the qi of spring causes disease located in the head; the qi of summer causes disease located in the viscera; the qi of autumn causes disease located in the shoulders and back; the qi of winter causes disease located in the four limbs. Thus in spring one is prone to nosebleeds and disorders of the nose; in midsummer one is prone to disorders of the chest and rib-sides; in late summer one is prone to diarrhea with undigested food and cold in the middle; in autumn one is prone to wind-malaria; in winter one is prone to impediment and reversal. Thus if in winter one does not disturb the yang qi through excessive exertion, then in spring one will not suffer nosebleeds, nor illness of the neck; in midsummer one will not suffer disorders of the chest and rib-sides; in late summer one will not suffer diarrhea with undigested food and cold in the middle; in autumn one will not suffer wind-malaria; and in winter one will not suffer impediment, reversal, or diarrhea with undigested food, nor abnormal sweating. Now essence is the root and foundation of the body. Hence one who stores the essence well will not suffer warm-disease in spring. If in summer one sweats but the heat is not released, in autumn this will develop into wind-malaria. This is the method of pulse diagnosis for the ordinary healthy person.'

Hence it is said: within yin there is yin, and within yang there is yang. From dawn to midday is the yang of Heaven — the yang within yang. From midday to dusk is also the yang of Heaven — but the yin within yang. From nightfall to the cock's crow is the yin of Heaven — the yin within yin. From the cock's crow to dawn is also the yin of Heaven — but the yang within yin. Thus the human being corresponds to this as well. Now, speaking of yin and yang in the human being: the exterior is yang and the interior is yin. Speaking of yin and yang within the human body: the back is yang and the abdomen is yin. Speaking of yin and yang among the viscera and bowels of the human body: the viscera are yin and the bowels are yang. The five viscera — liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney — are all yin. The six bowels — gallbladder, stomach, large intestine, small intestine, bladder, and triple burner — are all yang. The reason one wishes to know the yin within yin and the yang within yang is this: winter diseases are located in yin, summer diseases are located in yang, spring diseases are located in yin, and autumn diseases are located in yang — in each case one must observe where the disease is located, in order to apply needling or stone therapy accordingly. Thus: the back is yang; the yang within yang is the heart. The back is yang; the yin within yang is the lung. The abdomen is yin; the yin within yin is the kidney. The abdomen is yin; the yang within yin is the liver. The abdomen is yin; the utmost yin within yin is the spleen. All of these are the mutual correspondences of yin and yang, exterior and interior, inner and outer, male and female counterparts, and thus they correspond to the yin and yang of Heaven.

Huangdi said: 'The five viscera correspond to the four seasons — does each have its own particular correspondences?' Qibo said: 'They do. In the east, the color is green-blue; it enters and communicates with the liver, opens into the eyes, and stores its essence in the liver. Its diseases manifest as fright and panic. Its flavor is sour, its category is grass and trees, its domestic animal is the fowl, its grain is wheat. Among the four seasons it corresponds to spring, and above, to the planet Jupiter, the Year Star. This is why the qi of spring is felt in the head. Its note is jue, its number is eight, and thus one knows that its diseases are located in the sinews. Its odor is rank. In the south, the color is red; it enters and communicates with the heart, opens into the ears, and stores its essence in the heart. Hence its diseases are in the five viscera. Its flavor is bitter, its category is fire, its domestic animal is the sheep, its grain is glutinous millet. Among the four seasons it corresponds to summer, and above, to the planet Mars, the Sparkling Deluder. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the vessels. Its note is zhi, its number is seven, and its odor is scorched. In the center, the color is yellow; it enters and communicates with the spleen, opens into the mouth, and stores its essence in the spleen. Hence its diseases are at the root of the tongue. Its flavor is sweet, its category is earth, its domestic animal is the ox, its grain is panicled millet. Among the four seasons it corresponds to late summer, and above, to the planet Saturn, the Quelling Star. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the flesh. Its note is gong, its number is five, and its odor is fragrant. In the west, the color is white; it enters and communicates with the lung, opens into the nose, and stores its essence in the lung. Hence its diseases are in the back. Its flavor is pungent, its category is metal, its domestic animal is the horse, its grain is rice. Among the four seasons it corresponds to autumn, and above, to the planet Venus, the Great White. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the skin and body hair. Its note is shang, its number is nine, and its odor is fishy. In the north, the color is black; it enters and communicates with the kidney, opens into the two lower orifices, and stores its essence in the kidney. Hence its diseases are at the valley junctures of the flesh. Its flavor is salty, its category is water, its domestic animal is the pig, its grain is beans. Among the four seasons it corresponds to winter, and above, to the planet Mercury, the Star of Chen. Thus one knows that its diseases are located in the bones. Its note is yu, its number is six, and its odor is putrid. Thus, one who is skilled in reading the pulse carefully examines the five viscera and six bowels, their mutual accordance and opposition, the yin and yang, exterior and interior, and the ordered correspondences of male and female counterparts, and holds this in the mind, uniting the mind with the essence of the teaching. One should not teach this to a person unfit for it, nor transmit it to one who is not genuine — this is what is meant by attaining the Way.'
